coredata.dc:title: Self-assembly of porous cellulose fibers and the incorporation of graphene carbon quantum dots for stable luminescence
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xin
Cheng, Youliang
Wang, Sha
Fang, Changqing
Chen, Jing
Li, Mengyao
Zhao, Weina
coredata.dc:description: Porous fibers as excellent carriers can be used to prepare photoluminescence materials. Herein, cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) were derived from microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) by sulfuric acid hydrolysis. After CNCs were squeezed into a coagulating bath containing silicon precursors obtained by the hydrolysis of tetraethyl orthosilicate, porous cellulose fibers were constructed through self-assembly and then incorporated with graphene carbon quantum dots (GQDs) to prepare porous photoluminescence cellulose fibers. The silicon precursor amount, self-assembly time, and corrosion time were optimized. In addition, the morphology, structure and optical properties of the products were investigated. These results showed that as-prepared porous cellulose fibers with mesopores presented loose and porous mesh. Interestingly, the porous photoluminescence cellulose fibers exhibited blue fluorescence, and the maximum emission peak appeared at 430 nm under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Furthermore, the relative fluorescence intensity of the porous photoluminescence cellulose fibers was significantly enhanced compared with nonporous photoluminescence cellulose fibers. This work provided a new method to prepare environmentally and stably photoluminescence fibers, which had potential applications in anti-counterfeit packaging and smart packaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose nanocrystals
Porous fibers
Photoluminescence materials
Graphene quantum dots
Self-assembly

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of porous cellulose fibers with mesopores via self-assembly method
Morphology, structure and component of porous cellulose fibers with mesopores
Optical properties of porous photoluminescence fibers

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2021YFD1600402 ), Key Research and Development Plan of Shaanxi Province (Grant No. 2020GXLH-Z-031 ), Key Scientific Research Project of Weinan City (Grant No. 2020ZDYF-GYCX-107 ), Key Scientific Research Project of Education Department of Shaanxi Province (Grant No. 20JS106 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation illustration of mesoporous cellulose fibers by self-assembly method.
(a) SEM image of MCC, (b) TEM image of C1, (c) the length and (d) the diameter distribution histogram of CNCs, (e) XRD patterns and (f) FTIR spectra of MCC and C1.

SEM images for the surface of composite cellulose fibers obtained with the silicon precursor addition amount of 800 μL after different self-assembly time in the coagulation bath: (a) 3 h under high magnification, (b) 3 h under low magnification, (c) 6 h, (d) 9 h, (e) 12 h and (f) 18 h.
(a) SEM images, and (b) corresponding element maps of CS obtained with the silicon precursor addition amount of 800 μL after self-assembly of 12 h.

TG curves of composite fibers obtained after the HF treatment for different time and C1. The sample of CS was obtained with the silicon precursor addition amount of 800 μL after the self-assembly of 12 h.
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution of three porous cellulose fibers: (a) C2–400, (b) C2–600 and (c) C2–800.
(a) Digital picture, (b) SEM image under low magnification, (c) SEM image of surface morphology, (d) SEM image of cross section morphology, (e) TEM image and (f) XRD pattern for C2–800.
UV–vis spectra of (a) GQDs solutions and (b) porous photoluminescence fibers obtained after different impregnation time. C2 represents the sample of C2–800.

(a) TEM images of GQDs, (b) high resolution TEM image of GQDs, (c) TEM image of C3–30 through the combination of C2–800 with GQDs, (d) high resolution TEM image of C3–30, (e) FTIR spectrum of GQDs, (f) FTIR spectra of C2–800 and C3–30.

Digital photographs for porous cellulose fiber of C2–800 and photoluminescence fiber of C3–30 under (a) daylight and (b) UV irradiation, (c) UV–vis absorption spectra and (d) PL spectra for photoluminescence fibers of C3–30 and C4–30 (The insertion is PL spectra of GQDs).

(a) The stability of PL at different pH values, (b) resistance to photobleaching and (c) luminescence stability of porous photoluminescence fibers, (d) application of photoluminescence porous fiber in intelligent anti-counterfeiting packaging.

head.ce:title: Self-assembly of porous cellulose fibers and the incorporation of graphene carbon quantum dots for stable luminescence

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical and Precision Instrument Engineering, Xi'an University of Technology, Xi'an 710048, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical and Precision Instrument Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Printing, Packaging Engineering and Digital Media Technology, Xi'an University of Technology, Xi'an 710048, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Printing, Packaging Engineering and Digital Media Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: C. Fang, School of Mechanical and Precision Instrument Engineering, Xi'an University of Technology, Xi'an 710048, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical and Precision Instrument Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Porous fibers as excellent carriers can be used to prepare photoluminescence materials. Herein, cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) were derived from microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) by sulfuric acid hydrolysis. After CNCs were squeezed into a coagulating bath containing silicon precursors obtained by the hydrolysis of tetraethyl orthosilicate, porous cellulose fibers were constructed through self-assembly and then incorporated with graphene carbon quantum dots (GQDs) to prepare porous photoluminescence cellulose fibers. The silicon precursor amount, self-assembly time, and corrosion time were optimized. In addition, the morphology, structure and optical properties of the products were investigated. These results showed that as-prepared porous cellulose fibers with mesopores presented loose and porous mesh. Interestingly, the porous photoluminescence cellulose fibers exhibited blue fluorescence, and the maximum emission peak appeared at 430 nm under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Furthermore, the relative fluorescence intensity of the porous photoluminescence cellulose fibers was significantly enhanced compared with nonporous photoluminescence cellulose fibers. This work provided a new method to prepare environmentally and stably photoluminescence fibers, which had potential applications in anti-counterfeit packaging and smart packaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Compared with common fibers, porous fibers have higher surface roughness, surface activity, porosity and permeability. Until now, varieties of methods for preparing porous fibers include melt spinning, electrostatic spinning, freeze spinning, wet spinning and so on (Huang et al., 2016). These porous materials have been widely applied in adsorption and filtration, biological tissue engineering, sensors, photocatalysis and so on (Huang & Thomas, 2019). In decades, cellulose materials have been developed significantly to alleviate resource shortage and then support sustainable development (Klemm et al., 2005). Particularly, nanocellulose is suitable raw material of lightweight porous materials due to its high aspect ratio, large specific surface area, good modifiability, excellent mechanical strength, renewability and biodegradability (Sun et al., 2021). In addition, cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) can be combined with suitable precursors to form the composites, and then the CNCs template or precursors can be selectively removed to obtain mesoporous materials. According to this strategy, Karadagli et al. used CNCs and calcium thiocyanate tetrahydrate to prepare porous cellulose aerogel fibers via an ethanol coagulation bath along with twin-screw extrusion and supercritical CO2 drying, which of pore size was in the range of 10–100 nm (Karadagli et al., 2015).

Moreover, porous fibers as excellent carriers can be combined with stimulus-responsive functional materials and the obtained hybrid materials will undergo reversible changes in response to external stimulus such as light, heat and electricity (Terpstra et al., 2017). Specially, photoluminescence fibers as advanced materials have attracted the attention of many researchers (Tian et al., 2017). In recent years, some organic dyes such as spiro-pyran and azobenzene have been incorporated with fibers to achieve the photoluminescence of fibers (Abdollahi et al., 2018; Nakano et al., 2013), but these fibers have low fatigue resistance susceptible to the environment. Additionally, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are non-toxic, biocompatible and biodegradable nanomaterials (Danial, Mohamed, & Majid, 2021; Golmohammadi et al., 2017; Sabo et al., 2016), and they exhibit excellent photoabsorption, easy dispersion, high photostability and strong photoluminescence (Yan et al., 2019). Therefore, the combination of GQDs with nanocellulose also becomes an exciting issue. This strategy can endow as-prepared hybrid nanomaterials with excellent properties and functional surfaces (Danial, Md Bahri, & Abdul Majid, 2021). For example, Mahmoud et al. prepared drug analysis sensors with high electrical conductivity and good mechanical properties by doping nitrogen, sulfur and GQDs in nanocellulose (Mahmoud et al., 2020). Although the applications of GQDs in sensors (Jlassi et al., 2021), catalysis (Qian et al., 2017) and biomedicine have been investigated, few reports related to photoluminescence properties after combining GQDs with cellulose.

Cellulose-based fluorescent materials are playing an important role in various fields due to their easy processing, biodegradability, environmental friendliness, low cost and fast response time (Nawaz et al., 2021). Our central hypothesis is to use porous fibers with high specific surface area to load GQDs, thereby effectively exhibiting the photoluminescence. Although electrospinning is the most common method for preparing porous cellulose fibers, it usually needs to dissolve cellulose in organic solvents before spinning. The volatilization of organic solvents during the spinning process not only pollutes the environment, but also causes significant harm to human health. In addition, it is difficult to obtain dispersed fibers by using electrospinning. In contrast, wet spinning is simple and environmentally friendly because the coagulation bath usually uses ethanol or water effectively avoiding highly corrosive or toxic organic solvents. Importantly, this work not only utilized wet spinning to form dispersed fibers, but also used SiO2 particles as the template to build pores.

According to previous report, composite materials with stable photoluminescence properties can be prepared by combining the active groups of cellulose compounds with GQDs (Saleh Mohammadnia et al., 2022). Thus, CNCs was extracted from cellulose, and porous cellulose fiber with mesopores was prepared by combining wet spinning and self-assembly. The effects of self-assembly time, HF treatment time and silicon precursor dosage on the morphology and microstructure of porous fibers were investigated. In addition, environmentally friendly photoluminescence fibers by combining porous cellulose fibers with GQDs were obtained via a simple impregnation method. As-prepared photoluminescence fibers exhibited stable photoluminescence, which has potential application in anti-counterfeit packaging.

Microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) and concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4 95 %–98 %) (AR: Analytical regent) were purchased from Shengao chemical regent factory, Zhengzhou, China. Deionized water and GQDs were obtained from Shaanxi Dexiang Experimental Equipment Co., LTD. Hydrochloric acid (HCl) (AR) was bought from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) (AR) was supplied by Guangdong Guanghua Sci-tech Co., Ltd. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) (AR) and absolute ethyl alcohol (AR) were purchased from Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent. Hydrofluoric acid (HF) (AR) was bought from Shanxi Tongjie Chemical Reagent Co. LTD. All reagents are used directly without further treatment.

Typically, 23.2 g of MCC was added to a solution of concentrated H2SO4 with 64 wt% and stirred in a 45 °C water bath for 45 min. Then, the reaction was terminated by pouring an appropriate amount of deionized water into the system. After standing for 12 h, the nether milky white precipitate was collected before adding deionized water and then centrifuged until the upper liquid layer changed to turbid. Finally, the suspension was dialyzed, ultrasonically crushed and evaporated sequentially to obtain CNCs suspension with a concentration of 3 wt%. The extracted nanocellulose sample was denoted as C1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of porous cellulose fibers with mesopores via self-assembly method
ce:section.ce:para: TEOS was mixed with 0.1 mol/L HCl (a mass ratio of 2:1) and stirred for 1 h at room temperature to obtain the silica precursor solution (silicate solution). Typically, the coagulation bath was configured with 15 wt% NaOH solution (10 mL) and 95 wt% (10 mL) ethanol, and then certain amount of silica precursor solution was squeezed into it for the homogenization. Subsequently, 10 mL of CNCs (3 wt%) suspension was injected into the coagulation bath by wet spinning, and these CNCs were self-assembled into CNCs fibers due to the electrostatic repulsion and van der Waals forces. During the spinning process, the CNCs suspension should be extruded in the same direction at a constant speed and the CNCs/SiO2 composite fiber (CS) was obtained after self-assembling for 12 h and following by alcohol wash and drying. After CS was treated with 5 wt% HF for 25 min to remove SiO2, the products were thoroughly washed with anhydrous ethanol/deionized water mixture with a volume ratio of 1:1 and dried in vacuum oven to obtain porous cellulose fibers (C2). The preparation process of porous cellulose fibers is illustrated in Fig. 1 . To optimize the morphology and structure of porous cellulose fibers, the self-assembly time (3 h–18 h) of CNCs primary fiber and silica precursor, the soaking time (5 min–25 min) in HF and the addition amount of silica precursor (400 μL–800 μL) were changed. According to the addition amount of silica precursor, the sample C2 was denoted as C2–400, C2–600 and C2–800, respectively.

25 mg of C2 was immersed in 5 mL of GQDs solution (for 5–60 min) and then dried at room temperature to obtain porous photoluminescence fibers (C3). Photoluminescence CNCs fibers without silicon precursor (C4) were prepared as same as C3. For sample C4, the fiber was directly formed in the solidification bath without the participation of silicon precursor solution. These photoluminescence fibers were denoted as C3–x and C4–x, respectively, and x was immersing time.

The crystal structure and crystallization of samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction instrument (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance). The scanning rate was 8°/min in the range of 5°–60° with the Cu target. Cellulose I crystallinity was calculated by Segal empirical method (Mwaikambo & Ansell, 2002) and the calculation formula is shown in Eq. (1).
(1)
CI =
I 002
−
I am
/
I 002
× 100 %
where I
002 represents the diffraction intensity of the crystallization region (2θ = 22°); I
am represents the diffraction intensity of amorphous region (2θ = 18°) for cellulose I.

The specific surface area of mesoporous cellulose fibers was measured by a N2 adsorption-desorption instrument (APSP 2460 physical adsorption instrument) at a degassing temperature of 100 °C for 12 h. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR Nicolet iS 10) was used to record FTIR spectra in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 with KBr tablet. Thermogravimetric analysis of the products was performed on a thermogravimetric analyzer (TG NETZSCH 209F3) from 30 °C to 800 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C/min under N2 atmosphere. The field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM SU-8000 Hitachi) with an energy-dispersive spectrometer (EDS) attachment was used to observe the morphology of samples after sprayed with gold. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM FEI Tecnai F20) was used to observe the pore and the morphology of GQDs in mesoporous fibers. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV–vis 1800, Shimadzu Company, Japan) in the range of 200–800 nm. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained by a steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (PL HORIBA QuantaMaster 8000), and the emission spectra of samples were measured at the maximum excitation wavelength. The quantum yield of the photoluminescence fiber was determined by the fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS1000) under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Zeta potential was obtained by using nanoscale potentiometric analyzer (Malvern ZS90) and the surface charge density of CNCs was calculated according to Eqs. (2)–(4).
(2)
σ =
2 κε
ε 0
kT
z
e 0
sinh
z
e 0
φ 0
2 kT
+
2
κ
a 1
tanh
z
e 0
φ 0
4 kT
(3)
φ 0
= ς
1 + D /
a 1
e κD
(4)
a 1
=
3
d 2
L
16
3
where σ, κ −1, ε, ε 0, e 0, ς, D represent the surface charge density, Debye length, relative dielectric constant of medium, vacuum dielectric constant, electron change, zeta potential and distance from the sliding layer to the particle surface (typically 0.5 nm), respectively. In addition, k, T, a 1, L and d are Boltzmann's constant, absolute temperature, Stokes radius of the particle, the length and the diameter of the sample, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 2(a), many columnar MCC particles with the length of 10–60 μm and the diameter of about 15 μm were observed. Meanwhile, rod-shaped CNCs were formed after undergoing MCC acidolysis (in Fig. 2(b)). The average length and diameter of these CNCs were calculated according to 100 samples, which were 258.2 nm (Fig. 2(c)) and 20.5 nm (Fig. 2(d)), respectively. According to Eqs. (2)–(4), the surface charge density of C1 was calculated to 38.3 mC·m−2. Furthermore, these nanorods dispersed uniformly due to the electrostatic repulsion driven by negatively charged sulfate groups (-SO3−) on the surface of nanorods (Kian et al., 2018). Four distinct diffraction peaks at 2θ = 14.7°, 16.8°, 22.7° and 34.5° were corresponded to (101), (101), (002) and (040) crystal planes of cellulose I crystals (in Fig. 2(e)), indicating that the crystal structure of cellulose had few changes after the sulfuric acid hydrolysis (Jiang et al., 2010). According to Eq. (1), the calculated crystallinity of MCC and C1 was 80.1 % and 87.4 %, respectively, indicating that acid digestion mainly occurred in the amorphous region. Compared with MCC, the relative intensity of C1 at 2θ = 22° was obviously higher, which also proved that the crystallinity of the products increased after the hydrolysis.

As shown in Fig. 2(f), the absorption peak at 3346 cm−1 for C1 corresponds to the stretching vibration peak of -OH (Chen et al., 2019). Compared with MCC, the OH stretching vibration adsorption band for C1 was blue-shifted, implying that the intramolecular hydrogen bond changed from O (2) H⋯O (6) to O (3) H⋯O (5) (Oh et al., 2005). The absorption peak around 2900 cm−1 was ascribed to the CH stretching vibration of methyl groups and methylenes. The adsorption peak at 1631 cm−1 for C1 was associated with the bending vibration of H-O-H in adsorbed water molecules. It was relatively stronger compared with MCC, meaning that C1 had a higher capacity for adsorbing water molecules (Rhim et al., 2014). Additionally, the absorption peaks at 1163 cm−1, 1112 cm−1 and 1058 cm−1 for MCC and CNCs were corresponded to the glycosidic bond asymmetric stretching vibration and C-OH stretching vibration pyranose ring vibration, respectively (Kian et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, structure and component of porous cellulose fibers with mesopores
ce:section.ce:para: During the evaporation of water in the CNCs suspension, the rod-shaped CNCs undergoes self-assembled arrangement by intermolecular forces including electrostatic repulsion and van der Waals forces. To our knowledge, silica particles with a three-dimensional mesh structure were derived from TEOS through hydrolytic condensation and polymerization. Due to a large number of hydroxyl groups on the surface of silica particles and CNCs, CNCs/SiO2 composite cellulose fibers will be generated in situ by hydrogen bonding between silica particles and CNCs (Huang et al., 2019; Ruan et al., 2022).

CNCs primary fiber and silicon precursor underwent self-assembly after a certain time in the coagulation bath, and then hardened composite cellulose fibers containing SiO2 derived from silicon precursor were obtained. SEM images for the surface of composite cellulose fibers are shown in Fig. 3 . A small number of irregular SiO2 particles were obviously observed after the self-assembly of 3 h (in Fig. 3(a) and (b)). With the prolonging of self-assembly time, more SiO2 particles were formed in the composite cellulose fibers and gradually became near-spherical (in Fig. 3(c–e)). Interestingly, the CNCs fiber surface was densely and homogeneously covered with SiO2 particles when the self-assembly time was 12 h. However, when the self-assembly time extended to 18 h, more SiO2 were generated and led to Brownian motion, which made the self-assembly to be difficult and then the formation of irregular agglomerations due to the emergence of defects (Yang et al., 2011). Therefore, suitable self-assembly time positively affected the conversion of silicon precursors to SiO2 particles on the surface of composite cellulose fibers.

According to element maps of composite fiber (in Fig. 4 ), C, O, Si and Cl signals were observed, and Cl element was derived from HCl in silica precursor solution. In addition, the distribution of Si and O elements were consistent with the morphology of SiO2 in Fig. 4(a) and verified the components of particles on the surface of composite cellulose fibers in Fig. 3.

TG analysis was used to investigate the time for the complete removal of SiO2 from composite fibers by using HF, and the results are showing in Fig. 5 . The residual of C1 at 800 °C was about 27.9 wt%, and then we inferred that the content of SiO2 was about 28.3 wt% in CS according to the CS residual of about 56.2 wt%. The residual of CS after HF treatment for different time changed, it was near 27.9 wt% after HF treatment for 25 min where SiO2 was completely removed.

Fig. 6 illustrates the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution of porous cellulose fibers. The N2 adsorption - desorption isotherm for C2–400 was unclosed and crossed due to its very small specific surface area (in Fig. 6(a)). Obviously, N2 adsorption - desorption isotherms for C2–600 and C2–800 exhibited type IV with a hysteresis loop (in Fig. 6(b) and (c)). The capillary condensation occurred when the relative pressure of P/P0 exceeded 0.5, and the isotherm had an upward leap (Zhang et al., 2022). In addition, an H3-type hysteresis loop appeared, which could be caused by the sample's slit-like pores (Thommes et al., 2015). When the relative pressure approached to the saturation vapor pressure of P0, the pores were filled fully and the adsorption reached the saturation. The specific surface area for C2–400, C2–600 and C2–800 was 1.63 m2/g, 4.70 m2/g and 12.49 m2/g, respectively, which increased with the increasing of silica precursor amount. However, the specific surface area of the three samples was relatively low due to many macropores for C2 (Yu et al., 2018). In addition, the pore size for C2–400 according to BJH method was concentrated at near 2 nm and 6.3 nm, and these micropores may be attributed to the violent solidification of injected CNCs suspension in the coagulation bath and the slow condensation of internal polymer due to double diffusion. The pore size for C2–600 and C2–800 was concentrated at 28.8 nm and 20 nm respectively, and these mesopores are suitable for carrying GQDs.

As shown in Fig. 7(a)–(d), the fibers with a length of 10–15 cm and a diameter of about 300 μm consisted of porous mesh, and there were many macropores in C2–800 which is consistent with its low specific surface area. As shown in Fig. 7(e), it can be obviously observed that most mesopores were elliptical. These results indicate that it is significant to promote the formation of porous structure for cellulose fibers through the introduction of SiO2 particles and subsequent removal after the solidification. As shown in Fig. 7(f), three distinct diffraction peaks at 2θ = 12°, 20° and 22° were corresponded to the crystalline structure of cellulose II. The long soaking time of CNCs fibers in the coagulation bath led to the alkalinization of cellulose I in a concentrated NaOH solution and the cellulose crystalline type underwent the irreversible change (Gong et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of porous photoluminescence fibers
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 displays the UV–vis spectra of GQDs solution and porous photoluminescence fibers obtained by immersing C2–800 in GQDs solution. A prominent absorption peak was found around 350 nm for porous photoluminescence fibers except for C2–800 (in Fig. 8(b)), which was the typical absorption peak of GQDs (in Fig. 8(a)) due to the n-π* leap of CC and CO bonds (Wang, Lin, et al., 2021). Besides, the relative intensity of absorption peak at 350 nm for residue GQDs solution gradually decreased with the increasing of impregnation time (in the top right corner of Fig. 8(a)), while that for porous photoluminescence fiber gradually increased due to the amount increasing of adsorbed GQDs. The absorbance at about 350 nm for residue GQDs solution after the impregnation of 30 min showed a significant decline. Then, it changed a little bit, indicating that the adsorption of porous fiber to GQDs almost reached the saturation after the impregnation of 30 min. Therefore, the optimal impregnation time for porous cellulose fibers in GQDs solution was chosen to 30 min.

As shown in Fig. 9(a) and (b), spherical GQDs dispersed well and the diameter of most GQDs was in the range of 1–10 nm. Thus, mesopores with a centered size of 20 nm for the sample of C2–800 is suitable for carrying these GQDs. According to TEM image of C3–30, many spherical particles and a few mesoporous channels can be observed in porous cellulose fiber (in Fig. 9(c)). Additionally, the lattice spacing of individual spherical particles was 0.23 nm corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite (in Fig. 9(d)) (Sun et al., 2017), which was consistent with GQDs in Fig. 9(b) and further verified the successful combination of porous cellulose fiber with GQDs. Due to inherent hydroxyl groups for porous cellulose fibers, strong hydrogen bonds and dipole interactions will be formed between porous cellulose fibers and GQDs with hydroxyl and carbonyl groups. Therefore, GQDs easily attached to porous cellulose fibers and also can be anchored in the suitable pores of these fibers.

As shown in Fig. 9(e) and (f), a broad absorption peak band around 3450 cm−1 was attributed to the -OH stretching vibration, and the absorption peak at 1650 cm−1 was corresponded to the CO stretching vibration. In addition, the FTIR spectrum of C2–800 was similar to that for C3–30 and the absorption peak appeared nearly at the same location. However, the relative intensity of the absorption peaks at 3450 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 for C3–30 exhibited significantly higher compared with C2–800, which may be attributed to the addition of GQDs with many -OH and CO groups. According to TEM images and FTIR spectra, it was clear that the GQDs were successfully combined with porous cellulose fibers.

As shown in Fig. 10(a) and (b), both porous cellulose fiber of C2–800 and photoluminescence fiber of C3–30 appeared white without any difference under natural light, while only the sample of C3–30 emitted blue fluorescence under the irradiation of 365 nm light. In addition, the photoluminescence fibers of C3 still keep luminescent quality after long-term storage at room temperature. For the photoluminescence fibers of C3–30 and C4–30, the main adsorption peak in UV–vis spectra similarly located at about 350 nm (in Fig. 10(c)). Fig. 10(d) shows the fluorescence excitation spectra (EX dashed line) and emission spectra (EM solid line) of the photoluminescence fibers under the maximum excitation wavelength. Both of the excitation spectra for C3–30 and C4–30 were corresponded to the maximum excitation wavelength of 350 nm, which was consistent with the location of the UV–vis absorption peak. Under this excitation, the two fibers showed the characteristic emission peaks of GQDs around 430 nm, which agreed with the wavelength range of blue light. At the same time, it can be clearly seen from the insertion in Fig. 10(d) that the excitation and emission spectra of GQDs located at 354 nm and 453 nm which were almost consistent with PL spectra of photoluminescence fibers, indicating that GDQs were perfectly loaded into mesoporous fibers. Compared with C4–30, the relative intensity of fluorescence emission for C3–30 was higher due to additional GQDs adsorbed by porous C2–800. Thus, porous cellulose fibers with suitable mesopores can efficiently load GQDs to form photoluminescence fibers and further regulate the luminescence characteristics.

In addition, pH stability and luminescence stability of photoluminescence fibers have also been investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. 11(a). The relative fluorescence intensity of fibers was low at pH of 4 and 11, which was caused by GQDs aggregation after the addition of hydrochloric acid (Wang et al., 2017), the effects of protonation and deprotonation on functional groups (such as carboxylic groups) or defect sites in carbon cores (Zhao et al., 2016). For other pH values, the relative intensity of fluorescence for fibers changed little. These results proved that photoluminescence fibers exhibited good stability under most environment conditions. At the same time, the resistance of the composite fiber to photobleaching was detected by irradiating the sample under 365 nm light for 10 to 40 min. As shown in Fig. 11(b), the relative intensity of fluorescence for the composite fibers remained nearly consistent when the irradiation time exceeded 20 min. These results indicate that the prepared photoluminescence fibers not only exhibited good pH stability, but also had an excellent resistance to photobleaching.

Besides, the photoluminescence stability of the fiber was also investigated. During the storage time from first month to fourth months, the relative fluorescence intensity of the fiber slightly changed (Wang et al., 2017). Compared with the first month, the relative fluorescence intensity of photoluminescence fibers after the fifth and sixth months decreased by only 32.2 % and 39.3 %, indicating that as-prepared photoluminescence fibers could be stable for at least 4 months. According to testing, the quantum yield of as-prepared photoluminescence fiber in this work is 3.12 %. According to previous reports, the quantum yields of GQDs prepared by Wang and Pan et al. were 54 % and 32 % (Wang et al., 2017; Wang, Qi, et al., 2021) respectively, and they seemed to be much higher than the photoluminescence fibers in our work. Nevertheless, the luminescence stable time of GQDs in Wang and Pan's works were merely 40 days and 7 days, respectively. In contrast, the luminescence stability of our photoluminescence fibers is very high, and the luminescence stable time can be extended up to 4 months.

Based on PL properties of as-prepared cellulose fibers, they can be applied to smart packaging to support the safety of goods. Fig. 11(c) shows the application of photoluminescence fibers in packaging. Under the excitation of UV light, the areas containing photoluminescence fibers exhibited apparent patterns. This strategy not only improved the interest of consumers in goods but also provided an idea for applying anti-counterfeiting packaging.

This work prepared porous cellulose fibers via a self-assembly strategy by using CNCs as a substrate and TEOS as a silicon source at room temperature. Then, novel environmentally friendly photoluminescence fibers were obtained by loading GQDs into porous cellulose fiber with mesopores. The self-assembly time of CNCs in the coagulating bath containing silicon precursors had an important influence on the morphology of the composite fibers, and the optimal self-assembly time was 12 h when the amount of silicon precursor solution was chosen to 800 μL. The surface and cross section of porous cellulose fiber with a specific surface area of 12.49 m2/g showed porous mesh, and the pore size was concentrated at about 20 nm. As-prepared porous photoluminescence fibers emitted stable blue fluorescence under the irradiation of 365 nm light, and its relative fluorescence emission intensity was higher than photoluminescence fiber without silicon precursor. The PL property of porous photoluminescence fibers could be stable for at least 4 months. Therefore, this work not only prepared porous cellulose fibers for the carrier of function materials, but also provided a new strategy for synthesizing photoluminescence cellulose fibers.

Xin Zhang: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Youliang Cheng: Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Sha Wang: Validation, Investigation. Changqing Fang: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Jing Chen: Writing – review & editing. Mengyao Li: Formal analysis. Weina Zhao: Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2021YFD1600402), Key Research and Development Plan of Shaanxi Province (Grant No. 2020GXLH-Z-031), Key Scientific Research Project of Weinan City (Grant No. 2020ZDYF-GYCX-107), Key Scientific Research Project of Education Department of Shaanxi Province (Grant No. 20JS106).
